message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  We have successfully modeled every USA CoVID-19 wave using:
                  <jats:disp-formula id="ueqn1">
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="22270491v1_ueqn1.gif" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:disp-formula>
                  where
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ) is the total number of new CoVID-19 cases above a prior baseline, and
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  sets the doubling time
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>dbl</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  =
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  (ln 2). The new parameters {
                  <jats:italic>α</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ;
                  <jats:italic>δ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  } measure mitigation efforts among the
                  <jats:italic>uninfected population</jats:italic>
                  , with {
                  <jats:italic>α</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  > 0} being associated with
                  <jats:italic>Social Distancing</jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>vaccinations</jats:italic>
                  ; while {
                  <jats:italic>δ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  > 0 }is associated with
                  <jats:italic>mask-wearing</jats:italic>
                  , which gives a faster
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="22270491v1_inline1.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  post-peak
                  <jats:italic>drop-off</jats:italic>
                  . The predicted pandemic wave end is when
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ) no longer increases.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  Using data from 11/15/21-12/30/21, our prior
                  <jats:italic>medrxiv</jats:italic>
                  .
                  <jats:italic>org</jats:italic>
                  preprint* showed this initial
                  <jats:italic>Omicron</jats:italic>
                  CoVID-19 wave had values that matched the
                  <jats:italic>initial</jats:italic>
                  stage of the prior USA Winter 2020 resurgence {
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ≈8 05
                  <jats:italic>days</jats:italic>
                  ;
                  <jats:italic>α</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ≈ 0 011
                  <jats:italic>/ day</jats:italic>
                  }, when practically no one was vaccinated. In addition, this
                  <jats:italic>initial</jats:italic>
                  Winter 2021 wave showed virtually no
                  <jats:italic>mask-wearing</jats:italic>
                  {
                  <jats:italic>δ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>≈ ×</jats:italic>
                  0 001 10
                  <jats:sup>−3</jats:sup>
                  /
                  <jats:italic>day</jats:italic>
                  }, making it capable of infecting virtually everyone. These parameter values indicated that the
                  <jats:italic>Omicron</jats:italic>
                  variant was likely evading the vaccines in people who thought they were protected.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  As a result, stopping the
                  <jats:italic>Omicron</jats:italic>
                  CoVID-19 spread must once again rely on enhanced
                  <jats:italic>Social Distancing</jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>mask-wearing</jats:italic>
                  , just like the initial pandemic wave in March 2020. Analyzing the USA follow-on data from 12/25/21-1/31/22 shows that people did exactly that after the
                  <jats:italic>Christmas Holiday</jats:italic>
                  season, resulting in the following model parameters and values:
                  <jats:disp-formula id="ueqn2">
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="22270491v1_ueqn2.gif" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:disp-formula>
                  for this wave by itself, with all prior waves subtracted out as a baseline. Combining all the USA CoVID-19 waves gives these updated totals:
                  <jats:disp-formula id="ueqn3">
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="22270491v1_ueqn3.gif" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:disp-formula>
                  assuming no future CoVID-19 Resurgence (
                  <jats:italic>with 4 Figures</jats:italic>
                  ).
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  <jats:italic>*(10</jats:italic>
                  .
                  <jats:italic>1101_2021</jats:italic>
                  .
                  <jats:italic>10</jats:italic>
                  .
                  <jats:italic>15</jats:italic>
                  .
                  <jats:italic>21265078)</jats:italic>
                </jats:p>
title: USA Winter 2021 CoVID-19 Resurgence Post-Christmas Update

reference.unstructured: https://medrxiv.org/cgi/content/short/2020.05.04.20091207v1 “InitialModel for the Impact of Social Distancing on CoVID-19 Spread”
https://medrxiv.org/cgi/content/short/2020.06.30.20143149v1 “Orthogonal Functions for Evaluating Social Distancing Impact on CoVID-19 Spread”
https://medrxiv.org/cgi/content/short/2020.08.07.20169904 “Model to Describe Fast Shutoff of CoVID-19 Pandemic Spread”
https://medrxiv.org/cgi/content/short/2021.08.16.21262150 “The IHME vs Me: Modeling USA CoVID-19 Spread, Early Data to the Fifth Wave”
https://medrxiv.org/cgi/content/short/2021.10.15.21265078 “Updated Model for the USA Summer 2021 CoVID-19 Resurgence”
